source.title: Pressure Vessel Explosion at Loy-Lange Box Company

actions_taken: St. Louis Fire Department responded to the incident
OSHA investigated the incident and issued nine citations
CSB commissioned post-incident metallurgical examination of the failed SCR
SCR was removed from Faultless Healthcare Linen facility after coming to rest there
City of St. Louis adopted new ordinance 70800 in 2018 modifying inspection requirements
event.summary: On April 3, 2017, the bottom head of a Semi-Closed Receiver (SCR) pressure vessel at the Loy-Lange Box Company in St. Louis, Missouri catastrophically failed due to extensive oxygen pitting corrosion that had thinned the vessel wall to as little as 0.012 inches (from a nominal 0.250 inches). The failure caused a boiling liquid expanding vapor explosion (BLEVE), launching the SCR approximately 520 feet through the air into a neighbouring business (Faultless Healthcare Linen), fatally injuring four people: one Loy-Lange employee and three members of the public. Contributing factors included inadequate water treatment allowing dissolved oxygen into the steam system, an inadequate 2012 repair by Kickham Boiler and Engineering that left thinned corroded material in place, absence of a mechanical integrity or inspection program at Loy-Lange, failure to register the SCR with the City of St. Louis, and the City's missed opportunities to identify and inspect the vessel. The repair inspector employed by Arise accepted the non-conforming 2012 repair without witnessing the repair in progress.
recommendations: 2017-04-I-MO-R1: Loy-Lange to develop and implement a comprehensive safety management system including CCPS Guidelines for Risk Based Process Safety elements
2017-04-I-MO-R2: Loy-Lange to engage a qualified third-party to conduct a comprehensive review or audit of regulatory compliance practices
2017-04-I-MO-R3: Loy-Lange to implement an electronic records and data management system for critical company records, safety policies, and operational data
2017-04-I-MO-R4: City of St. Louis Board of Aldermen to revise Mechanical Code to adopt NBIC Part 2 for inservice inspection of boilers and pressure vessels
2017-04-I-MO-R5: City of St. Louis Board of Aldermen to revise Mechanical Code to require pressure vessel inspections by NBBI inservice (IS) commissioned inspector
2017-04-I-MO-R6: Mayor of City of St. Louis to distribute findings of this report to all licensed stationary engineers and registered boiler and pressure vessel owning/operating entities
2017-04-I-MO-R7: Arise to update company policies/procedures with prescriptive elements in repair and alteration inspection and acceptance process to prevent acceptance of non-conforming repairs
2017-04-I-MO-R8: National Board of Boiler and Pressure Vessel Inspectors to update NB-263 Rules for Commissioned Inspectors with prescriptive elements to prevent acceptance of non-conforming repairs
key_phrases: City of St. Louis inspection failures
pressure relief valve set above MAWP

hazards.name: Pressurised hot water in SCR above atmospheric boiling point (100 psig, 330°F)
Oxygen pitting corrosion in steam system pressure vessel

threats.name: Chronic oxygen pitting corrosion thinning SCR bottom head wall below minimum thickness
Inadequate 2012 repair leaving thinned corroded material in place (Kickham flush patch)
Inadequate water treatment allowing dissolved oxygen into steam system water
Daily startup practices introducing oxygenated water into SCR bottom head

consequences.name: Fatality - Loy-Lange employee killed by BLEVE at point of SCR failure
Fatalities - Three members of public killed when SCR projectile penetrated roof of Faultless Healthcare Linen
Structural damage to multiple buildings from SCR projectile and debris field
BLEVE blast wave and fragment throw causing widespread property damage

controls.name: Mechanical deaeration of make-up tank water (steam injection heating to 190-200°F)
controls.barrier_role: Remove dissolved oxygen from feedwater before introduction to SCR
controls.lod_basis: First engineered barrier against oxygen ingress into steam system

supporting_text: Loy-Lange mechanically deaerated the make-up tank water by injecting steam into the tank to heat the water to 190-200°F

the CSB could not review documentation or data indicating that the make-up tank water was effectively heated by steam and mechanically deaerated—which would occur at temperatures between 190-200°F—before the water was introduced to the SCR

In the event the makeup tank was not heated to the requisite temperature before the water was introduced to the SCR, oxygenated water would be fed to the SCR at this point during the steam system startup operation

controls.name: Chemical deaeration - sodium sulfite oxygen scavenger injection at make-up tank and SCR
controls.barrier_role: Chemically neutralise dissolved oxygen in steam system water
controls.lod_basis: Second engineered barrier against oxygen ingress, used in tandem with mechanical deaeration

supporting_text: Loy-Lange also chemically deaerated the steam system water by injecting sodium sulfite at two locations: the make-up tank and the SCR
A deviation below the minimum required oxygen scavenger concentration was recorded in 48 of the 88 records
Starting in roughly 2008, when AquaComp overtook Loy-Lange's water treatment business, nearly every measurement (32 of 34 records) recorded shows an insufficiently low concentration of sodium sulfite
Loy-Lange and its contractors did not effectively monitor, remove, or treat the dissolved oxygen in the steam generation system's water

controls.name: Daily water chemistry testing and logbook recording by stationary engineers
controls.barrier_role: Monitor water chemistry to detect oxygen scavenger deficiencies and adjust dosing
controls.lod_basis: Administrative monitoring control to verify effectiveness of chemical treatment

supporting_text: Clayton's feedwater treatment reference manual recommends at minimum a daily test of water chemistry

According to Loy-Lange employees and Aquacomp, the Loy-Lange stationary engineers performed this testing daily and recorded the results manually in a logbook, which was not recovered from the scene of the explosion
Nothing replaces frequent water testing, interpretation of the results, and adjustments in chemical feed rate when necessary

controls.name: Monthly water treatment contractor visits (Clayton / Aquacomp) for chemistry testing and dosing adjustment
controls.barrier_role: Independent periodic verification and adjustment of water treatment programme
controls.lod_basis: Third-party administrative oversight of water treatment effectiveness

supporting_text: Clayton, and later Aquacomp, visited Loy-Lange monthly, to perform water chemistry testing, adjust Loy-Lange's chemical injection pumps, provide advice to the Loy-Lange stationary engineers, and resupply Loy-Lange with treatment chemicals when required
the contractors did not continuously monitor the program
In May 2016, Aquacomp identified oxygen pitting corrosion in the piping between the make-up tank and the SCR

controls.name: SCR blowdown procedure (weekly drain of SCR bottom head)
controls.barrier_role: Drain stagnant oxygenated water trapped in SCR bottom head and replace with treated water
controls.lod_basis: Operational procedure to reduce oxygenated water contact time with bottom head

supporting_text: Clayton's 1995 operation manual recommended blowdown of the SCR weekly at minimum. In the absence of daily water tests, the manual requires a blowdown frequency of every 10-12 hours
Loy-Lange operators told the CSB that the vessel was rarely and irregularly blown down prior to 2012
it is likely that blowing down the SCR daily after startup would have drained oxygenated water from the bottom head and filled it with treated water

controls.name: Mechanical integrity and inspection programme for SCR (corrosion monitoring, UT measurement, internal inspection)
controls.barrier_role: Detect and monitor corrosion-induced wall thinning to enable timely intervention before failure
controls.lod_basis: Systematic inspection programme as recommended by NBIC Part 2 and API 510

supporting_text: The CSB found no evidence indicating that Loy-Lange had a formal inspection program that included internal visual inspection or non-destructive thickness measurement of the SCR separate from repairs
Loy-Lange did not have a robust mechanical integrity program that ensured the SCR was operating safely as intended and designed
An internal inspection or use of ultrasonic thickness measurement could have identified the SCR's corrosion and thinning
As early as 2004, Loy-Lange received recommendations from Clayton to perform an inspection of the SCR

controls.name: City of St. Louis annual pressure vessel inspection (regulatory jurisdictional inspection)
controls.barrier_role: Regulatory inspection to identify unsafe pressure vessel condition and enforce corrective action
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory third-party oversight as required by City of St. Louis Mechanical Code

supporting_text: The City of St. Louis was the regulatory authority responsible for inspecting pressure vessels in the city. However, due to manpower limitations and Loy-Lange's deficient regulatory compliance, the City never inspected the SCR

during the 20-year service life of the SCR (1997-2017), the city inspected the Loy-Lange steam system only four times: 1998, 1999, 2007 and 2010. None of the records produced include inspection records for the SCR
Resource limitations contributed to the City of St. Louis's irregular inspection of the Loy-Lange facility

controls.name: SCR registration and permitting with City of St. Louis (installation and repair permits)
controls.barrier_role: Ensure regulatory awareness of vessel existence to enable jurisdictional inspection and oversight
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory compliance prerequisite enabling jurisdictional inspection

supporting_text: Loy-Lange did not register the SCR with the City of St. Louis
The CSB confirmed that the City had no records of any kind for the SCR. The City had no installation permits, repair permits, or inspection records for the SCR
Loy-Lange did not ensure that the SCR was appropriately permitted as required by the City of St. Louis

controls.name: NBIC-compliant SCR repair by R-Stamp certified company (Kickham 2012 flush patch repair)
controls.barrier_role: Remove all corroded/thinned material and restore pressure vessel integrity to design standard
controls.lod_basis: Code-required repair standard to restore vessel to safe operating condition

supporting_text: Kickham likely left thinned material in place when it repaired the SCR bottom head in 2012, and in so doing did not adhere to the requirements of NBIC Part 3 Repair and Alterations

Destructive metallurgical cross-sectional examination shows that the reused ring was likely as thin as 0.101 inches at the time of repair in 2012. Notably, this would have been less than half of the required minimum thickness (0.2204 inches)
According to Kickham, it used visual examination and a measuring tape to verify the thickness of the plate. These methods, however, failed to detect the presence of the remaining corroded material
Only a portion of the corroded pressure vessel bottom head was replaced, leaving the compromised remaining portion of the head in place to continue corroding

controls.name: Authorized Inspection Agency (Arise) repair inspector oversight of 2012 SCR repair
controls.barrier_role: Independent verification that repair conforms to NBIC Part 3 requirements and all corroded material removed
controls.lod_basis: Independent third-party inspection required by NBBI R-Stamp repair process

supporting_text: Arise, the Authorized Inspection Agency for Kickham's repair, did not detect Kickham's inadequate repair
The Repair Inspector designated no inspection points, did not witness the repair in progress, and only witnessed the vessel's pressure test upon its return to service
Had the repair inspector detected and refused to accept Kickham's non-conforming repair, this incident could have been prevented

controls.name: Post-repair hydrostatic pressure test of SCR (110 psig, 2012)
controls.barrier_role: Verify leak-tightness of repair before returning vessel to service
controls.lod_basis: Code-required pressure test per NBIC Part 3 and City of St. Louis Mechanical Code

supporting_text: Kickham and Loy-Lange conducted a post-repair hydrostatic pressure test on the SCR prior to returning it to service. The test was conducted using water at 110 psig
The vessel passed a hydrostatic leak test at 110 psi

The 2011 edition of the NBIC Part 3 required in Paragraph 4.4.1.a.1 that the 'test pressure shall be the minimum required to verify the leak tightness integrity of the repair, but not more than 150%' of the vessel's MAWP, which would have been 225 psi for the Loy-Lange SCR

controls.name: Pressure relief valve on SCR (safety device to protect against overpressure)
controls.barrier_role: Relieve excess pressure to prevent vessel overpressure failure
controls.lod_basis: Last line of defence against overpressure per ASME BPVC and NBIC

supporting_text: the SCR was found to be equipped with a pressure relief valve set at 200 psi. The SCR typically contained water at around 100 psig
Equipping a pressure vessel with a relief valve set at a higher pressure than its MAWP does not adhere to ASME or NBIC requirements
OSHA cited Loy-Lange for 'failing to ensure pressure relief valves are set at or below the [MAWP]' of the SCR
Pressure relief devices are typically the last line of defense protecting a vessel from overpressure

controls.name: Process safety management system including hazard analysis, incident investigation, and management of change
controls.barrier_role: Systematic identification and control of process hazards to prevent major incidents
controls.lod_basis: Overarching management system barrier encompassing multiple process safety elements

supporting_text: Loy-Lange did not have a comprehensive process safety management system
Loy-Lange did not produce any formal company safety or operational policies or procedures regarding hazard analysis, incident investigation or mechanical integrity

Had Loy-Lange had a comprehensive process safety management system requiring the control and prevention of steam system corrosion, pressure vessel inspection, investigation of leaks, and pressure vessel repair quality assurance, the incident could have been prevented
Loy-Lange's process safety culture was such that the company normalized the occurrence of leaks in its steam system

controls.name: Incident investigation programme following prior SCR corrosion events (2004, 2012 leaks)
controls.barrier_role: Identify root causes of corrosion events and implement corrective actions to prevent recurrence
controls.lod_basis: Learning from experience element of process safety management

supporting_text: Loy-Lange never conducted an incident investigation when corrosion was found in the steam generation system
After repairing the leaking SCR, Loy-Lange did not conduct an incident investigation to determine the cause(s) of the failure
Loy-Lange could have prevented the 2017 incident if it had conducted an effective incident investigation after the 2004 or 2012 SCR corrosion failures

controls.name: Removal of leaking SCR from service pending inspection and repair (shutdown decision on March 31, 2017)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent operation of compromised vessel and avoid catastrophic failure
controls.lod_basis: Last opportunity mitigation - shutdown of leaking vessel before catastrophic failure

supporting_text: On Friday, March 31, 2017, Loy-Lange stationary engineers observed a leak that appeared to be coming from underneath the SCR
Loy-Lange continued operating the leaking pressure vessel throughout the remainder of the Friday production schedule and shut down the steam system as normal that evening
The following Monday a Loy-Lange stationary engineer began his normal start-up routine at about 6:00 a.m.
A robust mechanical integrity program should have identified a leaking pressure vessel as a safety issue that should have prevented a startup
We'd had this thing dripping and leaking like crazy before. So was it a concern? Yes, we wanted it repaired. But we weren't freaking out about it at the time

controls.name: Emergency response - St. Louis Fire Department response to explosion
controls.barrier_role: Limit consequences of explosion through emergency response and rescue
controls.lod_basis: Post-event emergency response to mitigate harm

supporting_text: The St. Louis Fire Department responded to the incident

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.

additional_pif_references.PIF-001: Stationary engineers lacked competence/awareness regarding effective mechanical deaeration verification and startup oxygen management
additional_pif_references.PIF-002: Inadequate procedures for startup oxygen scavenger dosing and blowdown frequency; operators rarely performed blowdown prior to 2012
additional_pif_references.PIF-003: Organisational safety culture normalised leaks; management did not understand process hazards; no process safety management system
additional_pif_references.PIF-004: No training or procedures for hazard analysis, incident investigation, or mechanical integrity at Loy-Lange
additional_pif_references.PIF-005: City of St. Louis resource limitations and de-prioritisation of boiler/pressure vessel inspections contributed to regulatory oversight failure
additional_pif_references.PIF-006: Kickham repair inspector (Arise) did not designate inspection points or witness repair in progress; relied solely on post-repair hydrostatic test
additional_pif_references.PIF-007: Kickham used inadequate measurement tools (tape measure, visual inspection) instead of calipers or UT measurement to verify wall thickness
additional_pif_references.PIF-008: Situational awareness failure - operators and management did not recognise leaking pressure vessel as indicator of compromised integrity requiring shutdown
